Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Configuring and demonstrating flexibility and speed in adding or updating edits, workflows, integrations, business rules, reference data, and related configuration artifacts needed to achieve target performance, accuracy, and variance metrics.
5. Demonstrating security compliance and an ATO-ready posture appropriate to the PoC environment.

This procurement will be conducted as a challenge-based acquisition with three phases. Feedback will be continuously provided throughout the phase. After each phase CMS will down-select vendors.
1. Request for Proposal (RFP) Acquisition using the evaluation criteria described in the solicitation.
2. Proof of Concept (Base Period, ending Jan 1 '27): CMS anticipates not more than four participants. 3. Production implementation (Option Periods as described in the Solicitation): One vendor only. The selected awardee will complete installation, integrate with all relevant external systems, and process CMS claims data according to its rollout plan.

Phase III PoC (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
The PoC will evaluate the offeror's ability to successfully demonstrate the integrated technical solution presented in Phase II. The demonstration must incorporate the capabilities described across Subfactors 2.1 through 2.5. A single 'Acceptable' or 'Unacceptable' rating will be assigned based on the overall success of the demonstration as measured against the Government's test plan. To be considered 'Acceptable', the offeror's solution must successfully demonstrate all mandatory pass/fail criteria identified in the test plan. Failure to meet any single mandatory criterion will result in an 'Unacceptable' rating for the Proof of Concept.

a. Subfactor 1: Offerors must brief a network design showcasing their approach to designing an enterprise network supporting multiple data centers as well as cloud environments utilizing the proposed solutions to meet the requirements of PWS Section 6.1 and PWS Appendix 5. The purpose of the network design is to demonstrate the offeror's approach to the proposed solution within the context of a Data Center network. The offeror may include network diagrams of the proposed design within the presentation slides as visual aids. Offerors may reference manufacturers' published data sheets for network components to demonstrate how solutions are meeting the requirements of PWS Appendix 5. The offeror must include the OEM data sheets for the devices used in the network design; the data sheets must be included as attachments/handouts to the presentation. The network design must use the criteria below.

(1) Design a network for two data centers, each with the following requirements:
(a) Traditionally hosted compute:
(i) Support for 1000 physical hosts per data center.
(ii) Support for 3000 virtual hosts.
(iii) Support 10Gbps throughput per host interface.
(iv) Future support of up to 100Gbps per host interface.
(v) Logical separation and control of Production and Management with further separation of Web, Database, App, Infrastructure Management, Enterprise Services, Data Replication, and Backup functions.
(vi) Solution must support micro segmentation and Zero Trust.
(vii) Support for fully architected enclaves requiring only external connectivity.
(2) Provide secure connectivity between two data centers.
(3) Provide secure connectivity between each data center and one or more commercial cloud environments.

Digital Competency Management System

The FAA intends to conduct a ChBA as shown below in Table 1.

Phase 3: Challenge – Modernization Factory Execution
Description: FAA will evaluate Offerors' ability to actually demonstrate their tools and approach, and execute a modernization effort. Additional details will be provided to all Offerors invited to Phase 3.
Evaluation Criteria: Phase 3 responses will be due appr 9-12 weeks from FAA notice.

Based on the demonstrated capabilities in Phase 1-3 and proposed pricing, the FAA intends to select one or more Offeror for contract award. Contract award(s) by September 30, 2026.

The FAA anticipates an accelerated schedule across the phases. For Phase 1, interested parties must submit responses no later than March 13, 2026 by 5PM (ET). Responses will be evaluated as described in Section 5.

After evaluating Phase 1 responses, the FAA will make a down-select decision regarding which Offerors will be invited to Phase 2 as detailed in Section 5.b. All Offerors will be provided notice of the FAA's decision. If invited to participate in Phase 2, the respondent must be prepared to participate in an Industry Day within one (1) to two (2) weeks from notification and submit a Phase 2 response within two (2) to three (3) weeks from the Industry Day.

2.2.2.2 Subfactor 2b: Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation

Product Demonstration/Oral presentations shall be provided using Microsoft PowerPoint. The number of slides should not exceed 45. Offerors are instructed to submit their PowerPoint presentation with their proposal. Offerors will not have an opportunity to revise the PowerPoint after the submission due date has closed.

Offerors will be contacted about scheduling the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation. The Government anticipates the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation to take place within two (2) weeks from the proposal submission due date. The Government reserves the right to reschedule the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation at the sole discretion of the Contracting Officer. Government attendees will be identified at the time of the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation. If an Offeror fails to schedule the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation as instructed, the Offeror's proposal will not be evaluated and will make the Offeror ineligible for award.

The location for the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation will be at Joint Base Andrews, Maryland or Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling, DC. The Government reserves the right to change the site at the sole discretion of the Contracting Officer. Offerors may not present any classified information in either their proposals or their Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation.

During the Offeror's Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation, they will be given time to set up and then be allowed 60 minutes to demonstrate the live capabilities of their product. Offerors are instructed to demonstrate the objectives labeled 'presentation' in Table 2 above.

Presentations must be done in person and no more than two presenters per Offeror. Offerors shall not use any type of video during the oral presentation, whether as pre-recorded presentations or as part of the Offeror's briefing materials. No external consultants will be allowed to represent an Offeror in the product demonstration.

Note 1: The Government reserves the right to ask questions after the 60-minute presentation following a break for consultation. The Questions & Answers (Q&A) session will be considered in the Offeror's technical evaluation. Q&A does not constitute formal discussions.

Note 2: IAW RFO 15.202(a)(3), Oral Presentations will be recorded. If an Offeror refuses to have their Oral Presentation recorded, they will be determined Unacceptable and not further evaluated.

2.2.2.1 Subfactor 2a: Live Area/Topic Specific Demonstration (.csv Submissions)

The Fly-Off will consist of several phases:
a. Solicitation Phase (specific Call procedures are explained in Section 7)
i. Pitch-Deck Submission and Evaluation (which includes an eligibility determination)
ii. Request for Proposal
iii. Proposal Evaluation
b. Contract Award
i. Negotiation
ii. Other Transaction for Prototype Award
c. Test Planning
i. Adjudication of any required modifications to base Fly-Off test plan based on specific system under test.
d. System Integration onto Autonomy Prime Testbed Aircraft
i. Integration of test subject hardware + software into Autonomy Prime Testbed (Interface Control Document (ICD) and Application Program Interface (API) will be provided during RFP phase of solicitation) at Eglin AFB, FL.
ii. Ground Checks of the system completed.
e. Flight test events
i. The Autonomy Prime testbed will attempt to fly predetermined course(s) using navigation solution(s) provided by the test system. Divergence from optimal course will be allowed and monitored up to a predetermined geofence. Error from optimal course will be measured.
f. System Disassembly from Autonomy Prime Testbed Aircraft
i. Test subject hardware + software should be removed from the Autonomy Prime Testbed and the Testbed returned to its original configuration.
ii. Inspection by the USAF test team to ensure the Testbed configuration has returned to its baseline and all performer assets have been returned.

B.4. Problem Statement:
Applicants will present an agentic AI tool that integrates advanced machine learning, natural language processing (NLP), behavioural analytics, and influence strategies.

The tool should possess capabilities to autonomously identify target audiences, craft personalised messaging, and adapt strategies in real time based on feedback and changing societal contexts, all while staying aligned with NATO's principles and ensuring legitimacy is always maintained.

Ideally the solution would have a Technology Readiness Level (TRL) of 5-7; with the possibility of immediate Minimum Viable Product (MVP) delivery preferred (under 3 months).

B.5. Scope
In General: The Agentic AI Tool should have the following characteristics:
i. Enhanced support to Decision-Making: Show the AI Tool analysing available data, identifying issues, and making actionable recommendations.
ii. Problem Solve: Allow the AI Tool to address a complex, multi-step task or find creative solutions under constraints for creating and managing channels of delivery, formulating effective messaging, and amplifying communication efforts.
iii. Supports Strategic Communication by:
Increasing tempo without sacrificing structure
Reducing analyst and coordination overload
Expanding planning option space
Creating persistent situational awareness
Enabling continuous adaptation
iv. Human-led judgment: Importantly, it should be framed as: Human-led judgment + AI-enabled scale and synthesis not autonomous influence decision-making.
v. Learn and Adapt: Incorporate unforeseen variables or novel situations during the demonstration to showcase the AI adjusting on the fly.

Challenge cycles will occur on a periodic basis beginning with an API check where Proposers will be evaluated on their ability to create API-compliant DTs and AI Test Agents, to validate their software development capabilities.

1. DT Challenges: Proposers will be provided with either as-flown or simulated flight test maneuvers. The Proposers' DTs will be evaluated on the accuracy of the predictions and uncertainty quantification for pre-defined engineering quantities of interest, relative to either hold-out flight test data or simulated truth data. Given further hold-out flight test data or simulated truth data, the Proposers' DTs will be evaluated on their ability to assimilate new data and subsequently improve future predictions with reduced uncertainty for pre-defined engineering quantities of interest. In addition, the Proposers' DTs will be evaluated on the speed of their predictions, uncertainty quantification, and data assimilation.

2. AI Test Agent Challenges: Proposers will deliver AI Test Agents that will be integrated with a Government-supplied, uncertainty quantifying DT. The Proposer's AI Test Agents will be evaluated on their ability to generate safe, information-maximizing test maneuvers as the underlying DT learns, in real time. Specifically, the AI Test Agents will be evaluated on the degree to which they comply with user-defined constraints (fuel use, dynamically changing airspace, etc.), identify and avoid unsafe conditions consistent with user-defined risk tolerance, and the efficiency with which they achieve uncertainty reduction targets.

3. CyPhER Challenges: Proposers will deliver DTs and AI Test Agents for integration within the IV&V environment. The combined DT and AI Test Agents, called CyPhERs, will be evaluated on their ability to generate safe, information-maximizing test maneuvers as the underlying DT learns in real time. Specifically, CyPhERs will be evaluated to the degree to which they comply with user-defined constraints, identify and avoid unsafe conditions consistent with user-defined risk tolerance, the efficiency with which they achieve uncertainty reduction targets, and the accuracy of their predictions and uncertainty quantification regarding pre-defined engineering quantities of interest.

Step Two – Detailed Approach:
After White Paper evaluation(s), selected offerors will be invited to design and implement a solution for AMC's IT challenges for step two. Air Force subject matter experts (SMEs) will be available for consultation. The proposed solution must provide a detailed technical approach that meets or exceeds the following requirements:

A. Assessment & Analysis Methodology:
• Describe the technical approach for conducting a comprehensive assessment of the existing IT infrastructure at two representative AMC installations.
• This must include a detailed methodology for evaluating legacy systems to identify specific operational inefficiencies, performance bottlenecks, and security vulnerabilities.
• Proposals must specify the tools, diagnostic techniques, and personnel expertise that will be used to map the current "as-is" architecture and operational workflows.
• Explain how findings will be analyzed and documented to establish a clear performance and security baseline for the transition roadmap.

B. Proposed Future State Architecture:
• Present a high-level concept for the target "to-be" architecture that is secure, scalable, and resilient. This design must encompass core data centers, edge computing environments, and cloud components (hybrid and/or multi-cloud).
• Specifically address the approach for implementing a secure and accredited Cross-Domain Solution (CDS) to enable seamless data exchange between NIPRNET, SIPRNET, and other required domains.
• Describe the strategy for migrating legacy applications and on-premises data to the proposed modernized environment while ensuring mission continuity and adhering to strict cybersecurity requirements.

3.2. Evaluation Stages
The proposal evaluation process will lead to the identification of a finalist or finalists and will take place in two stages:
1. Evaluation of Proposal responses;
2. Interviews/Equipment and Service demonstration.

Upon completion of each stage in the process, the Committee will score each Proposer and select those who are short listed for the subsequent stage.

3.3.2. Stage 2, Interviews/Demonstrations.
A short-list of firms may be invited to Stage 2, Interview/Demonstration, step of the evaluation process. Interview/demonstrations are an important aspect of the evaluation process that offers the City an opportunity to see how the Proposer's solution meets the critical components of the RFP. Evaluation of Respondents in Stage 2 consists of oral interviews and presentations by Proposers short listed in Stage 1, along with an in-person equipment demonstration and testing of proposed services. One hundred (100) points will be the maximum score for Stage 2. Interviews and presentations may be conducted in-person, virtual, or some combination thereof. Proposers invited to participate in Stage 2 will be given additional information regarding the City's desired content in a reasonable time before the scheduled Stage 2 oral interviews and presentations are held.

For equipment and service demonstration, Proposers shall provide at least two (2) vehicles for each type of Fleet Vehicle offered for the proposed service. Evaluation standards include verification of the Proposers' ability to deliver proposed services with available equipment, and verification of the Proposers' proposed solutions to provide equitable access to qualifying users.

1. Equipment Demonstration: The mobile phone app, RFID card, keypad, text-to-unlock and/or any other method for reserving, unlocking and/or holding must be functional and presented for testing by the Committee to be considered as part of the Equipment Demonstration score.

Compliance with the City's safety requirements and any proposed safety technology, including, but not limited to, geofencing, speed governing, and sidewalk detection features, must be functional and presented for testing by the Evaluation Committee and will be considered as part of the Equipment Demonstration score.

If the Proposer includes any new or enhanced parking technology in its proposal, a demonstration of such technology must be provided as part of the Equipment Demonstration.

If the Proposer proposes features such as audible or visual warnings, detection of sidewalk riding, detection of multiple riders on a single device, or similar rider behavior monitoring technologies, these features must be demonstrated during the Equipment Demonstration.

The Equipment Demonstration factors will include riding on different surfaces, flat ground, inclines, accelerating, stopping, turning, starting, stopping, handlebar height adjustments, and other riding factors.

FACTOR 1: CHALLENGE SCENARIO

Offerors shall provide a (1) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 - Challenge Scenario 1a) as well as a (2) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 - Challenge Scenario 1b) based on both of the following scenarios.

The Program Executive Office Defense Healthcare Management Systems (PEO DHMS) is modernizing its global healthcare capabilities, which includes the multi-faceted deployment of DHMS products (Federal Electronic Health Record (EHR) and Operational Medicine (OpMed)). The objective is to create a secure, seamless, and interoperable health information ecosystem that supports warfighters from the point of injury in austere, forward-deployed locations to definitive care.

Offerors shall propose an innovative and scalable deployment plan addressing two interconnected lines of effort as outlined below. The proposed approach shall address hardware and/or component installation and configuration across various networks, continuous patient data access, and comprehensive user training tailored to the operational environment and a diverse user audience. Additionally, the plan shall incorporate proactive risk mitigation strategies to address deployment complexities and ensure mission success. Offerors shall document any assumptions made within their proposed approach.

1a) Federal EHR product modernization and deployment: This effort focuses on the full implementation of the Federal EHR at established, high-capacity treatment facilities.

Scope: One large accredited Outside Continental U.S. (OCONUS) medical center (250 beds), including two community hospitals (125 beds) and five associated out-patient clinics spread across multiple time zones with approximately 5,000 total users that includes foreign national employees and 50,000 beneficiaries.

The Government will conduct two separate evaluations of the offeror's proposed Deployment Approach (A003): one for Volume 1: Factor 1 - Challenge Scenario 1a and one for Volume 1: Factor 1 - Challenge Scenario 1b. To be rated Acceptable, the offeror's proposal must meet the following minimum requirements for each applicable sub-factor:

Sub-Factor 1: Program Management and Governance
The proposal must describe a program management and governance approach that:
1. Includes a framework for managing, monitoring, and controlling all activities described in the challenge scenario.
2. Addresses procedures for governance, risk management (identification, analysis, and mitigation), integrated scheduling, quality control, and stakeholder communication.
3. Addresses the unique management challenges of supporting concurrent deployments in fixed-facility (OCONUS medical center) (1a) or operational/expeditionary environments (1b).

Sub-Factor 2: Deployment and Integration
The proposal must describe a deployment and integration approach that:
1. Includes a methodology for site preparation and configuration (including data collection, migration, and integration).
2. Includes a methodology for ensuring ongoing compliance with accrediting agencies (as it applies to 1a).
3. Addresses integration with hardware, medical devices, and networks (as it applies to 1a).
4. Addresses integration with hardware, medical devices, and diverse Service-specific networks (as it applies 1b).
5. Includes a methodology for deployment in disconnected, intermittent, and low-bandwidth (DIL) environments (as it applies to 1b).
6. Presents at least one option for a low-to-no-touch remote deployment for OpMed products (as it applies to 1b).

Place of Performance: Monitoring may occur between Cochiti Dam and Elephant Butte Reservoir in the Cochiti, Angostura, Isleta, and San Acacia reaches.

The primary objective of this contract is to provide up to daily monitoring of river conditions to support river managers in their operations when there is the potential for river intermittency, generally from April to October each year. Monitoring may occur between Cochiti Dam and Elephant Butte Reservoir in the Cochiti, Angostura, Isleta, and San Acacia reaches (see attached map; these areas correspond to the Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District's Cochiti, Albuquerque, Belen, and Socorro Divisions) when gauged flows indicate that river drying is imminent or has already occurred. The majority of the work is anticipated to occur in the Isleta and San Acacia reaches.

The Contractor shall provide all personnel, equipment, tools, materials, vehicles, supervision, and other items and services necessary to safely complete the tasks defined in this Performance Work Statement. The Contractor shall perform to the standards in the contract.

NMCSD is a 24/7 critical medical facility supporting active-duty service members, veterans, and their families. The facility requires an on-site generation system capable of delivering uninterrupted utilities (electrical power, water, steam, compressed air) to critical care areas, operating rooms, and life-support. The MEGS must seamlessly integrate all utility systems without operational downtime, while providing redundant and load-balanced power generation with automated fault detection and correction. The risk of failure to meet these mission critical requirements to the support Naval Medical Center San Diego would pose unacceptable risk to the health and safety of patients and Government personnel. The incumbent contractor, Energy Technology and Service, Inc. (ETS), has been the primary provider of MEGS support. Consequently, ETS has unique and specialized knowledge of the system to ensure mission critical requirements are met. No other contractor could provide the localized knowledge and immediate services necessary to operate and maintain the existing MEGS System without a significant lead time and unacceptable delay. Such a delay would result in serious injury to the Government by creating an undue risk of loss of vital services at the Naval Medical Center San Diego.

Strategic Weapons Facility, Pacific (SWFPAC) requires in-person, instructor-led training for 15 personnel on VMware vSphere. The agency's server infrastructure and software-defined data center (SDDC) are standardized on the VMware vSphere 7 platform. This course provides the essential foundational knowledge for operating and maintaining VMware technologies within a software-defined data center environment. Because vSphere is the core virtualization platform upon which most other VMware solutions depend, this training is critical to ensuring SWFPAC personnel can effectively support, troubleshoot, and optimize the command's virtual infrastructure.

2. SCOPE
The contractor shall provide and deliver the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) authorized commercial training course, VMware vSphere: Install, Configure, Manage [V7]. The purpose of this training is to provide SWFPAC employees with the specific skills needed to manage the agency's standardized VMware virtual infrastructure.

The contractor shall provide a complete training package that includes the following:
2.1 Training Session: A single, five-day, in-person, instructor-led training session conducted onsite at SWFPAC facilities for up to fifteen (15) students.
2.2 One (1) currently certified VMware Instructor (VCI) to lead the training.

Statement of Work
Cybersecure HM&E Control Systems & Networks Department
Scrum Master and Product Owner Training

1.0 INTRODUCTION
1.1 The Naval Surface Warfare Center Philadelphia Division (NSWCPD) is a Department of War entity responsible for research and development, test and evaluation, engineering and fleet support organization for the Navy's ships, submarines, military watercraft and unmanned vehicles. This requirement is for NSWCPD Dept 50, which is responsible for Cybersecure Hull, Machinery & Electronic (HM&E) Control Systems & Networks Department.

1.2 BACKGROUND
The Cybersecure Machinery Controls Systems & Networks Department is the principal provider of life cycle management (LCM) and full spectrum engineering support for cybersecure surface ship machinery information, sensors and control systems; mission-critical networks; steering and integrated bridge/navigation systems. Scrum has been chosen as a framework across multiple programs to manage requirements and tasks associated with those requirements.

1.3 SCOPE OF WORK
The Vendor shall provide a three-day course on site at NSWCPD that will provide training in the roles of Scrum Team, Registered Scrum Master and Registered Product Owner. At the completion of the course and satisfactory participation by the individual attendees, the vendor will provide each attendee with both a Registered Scrum Master and Registered Product Owner certification exam password.

2.0 PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
The Vendor shall provide access to the training services no later than 30 days upon Award.

7.3.1-17 - Lowest Price Technically Acceptable Evaluation Method - November 2025
(a) After receipt and evaluation of proposals, FDIC may award a contract to the responsive offer that is the lowest priced and technically acceptable.
(b) After obtaining all pricing information, the FDIC may, at its discretion, limit the evaluation to the lowest priced offers or proposals. In such cases, additional proposals may not be evaluated. The FDIC also reserves the right to request additional price reductions from the lowest priced technically acceptable offeror, if it is in the FDIC's best interest.
(c) Technical acceptability will be determined by the offer meeting the FDIC's minimum technical requirements. Proposals will be evaluated as either "acceptable" or "unacceptable". If the offer cannot meet the minimum requirements of the solicitation, the offer will be deemed "unacceptable". There is no evaluation benefit for an offer that exceeds the minimum requirement.

SECTION M – EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1 Basis for Award
The Government intends to award a single contract to the responsible offeror whose quotation is determined to be Technically Acceptable and offers the Lowest Price. The Government will evaluate quotations and may make award without discussions. Therefore, the offeror's initial quotation should contain its best terms.

M.2 Evaluation Approach
The Government will use a Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical quotations will be evaluated on a pass/fail basis. Price will not be evaluated for tradeoff against technical factors.

M.3 Technical Acceptability
To be rated Technically Acceptable, the quotation must clearly demonstrate compliance with all requirements of the solicitation, including:
- Relevant experience performing similar facility condition assessments
- Qualified personnel, including a licensed Professional Engineer (PE)
- Technical approach that meets all Statement of Work requirements
- Ability to meet the required schedule
Failure to meet any of the above criteria will result in a rating of Technically Unacceptable, and the quotation will not be considered for award.

M.4 Price Evaluation
Price will be evaluated for completeness and reasonableness in accordance with FAR 13.106-3.

BASIS FOR AWARD: This award selection will be conducted using Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) procedures. To be eligible for an award, a proposal must meet all technical requirements, comply with all required terms and conditions, and include all required information. All non-price evaluation factors must be rated as "ACCEPTABLE" in order to be eligible for award. An "UNACCEPTABLE" rating in any factor will result in the proposal being rated "UNACCEPTABLE" unless corrected through discussions. A proposal rating of "UNACCEPTABLE" makes a proposal ineligible for award. Since the LPTA methodology prohibits trade-offs, all evaluation factors and sub-factors are equally important. Proposals are evaluated for acceptability but not ranked based on non-price factors.

A price evaluation will be performed for all offerors, and proposals will be ranked from lowest to highest price based on the Total Proposed Price (TPP) provided by the offeror on the price schedule sheet. Next, each factor will be evaluated and, subsequently, the overall proposal will be prepared, beginning with the lowest-priced proposal. The award will be made to the lowest-priced proposal that meets the acceptability standards for the non-price factors.

I. Technical: Quotes that meet all specifications outlined in this RFQ shall be considered technically acceptable. In accordance with FAR 52.212-1 and its addendum, all quotations must comply with the size requirements specified therein. Technical evaluations will be conducted on an acceptable/unacceptable basis.

II. Price Reasonableness: Will be conducted in accordance with FAR 12.203(a)(3) and FAR 12.203(c)(2). The total quoted price, to include shipping costs, will be used in the price evaluation.

III. Past Performance: Past performance will be evaluated to determine recency and relevancy. Past performance will be graded as acceptable or unacceptable and will not be weighted. No past performance will be considered as an acceptable rating.

PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.

Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

BASIS FOR AWARD
This acquisition is being conducted in accordance with FAR 13.5 along with procedures found within FAR 12 and FAR 15. This is a best value acquisition where the source selection process utilized is Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA). Evaluation of quotations will be IAW FAR 52.212-2.
Following the LPTA evaluation process:
The Government will first evaluate price to determine the lowest through highest priced quotations. Only the lowest priced quote will be evaluated for technical acceptability, unless the lowest priced quote is deemed technically unacceptable. If the lowest priced quote is deemed technically unacceptable, the next lowest quote will be considered, and so on until a quoter is identified to be technically acceptable.
Contractors are advised that the Government may not evaluate the technical quotation response of all Contractors under this RFQ.

7.3.1-17 - Lowest Price Technically Acceptable Evaluation Method - November 2025
(a) After receipt and evaluation of proposals, FDIC may award a contract to the responsive offer that is the lowest priced and technically acceptable.
(b) After obtaining all pricing information, the FDIC may, at its discretion, limit the evaluation to the lowest priced offers or proposals. In such cases, additional proposals may not be evaluated. The FDIC also reserves the right to request additional price reductions from the lowest priced technically acceptable offeror, if it is in the FDIC's best interest.
(c) Technical acceptability will be determined by the offer meeting the FDIC's minimum technical requirements. Proposals will be evaluated as either 'acceptable' or 'unacceptable'. If the offer cannot meet the minimum requirements of the solicitation, the offer will be deemed 'unacceptable'. There is no evaluation benefit for an offer that exceeds the minimum requirement.

ADDENDUM TO 52.212-2
Paragraph (a) is hereby replaced with the following:
a. The Government will issue an award resulting from this solicitation to the responsible contractor whose quote meets or exceeds the minimum technical requirements of the solicitation and provides fair and reasonable pricing.
b. Award will be on an all or none basis. The following adjectival rating will be used in evaluating each offeror's technical capability quote:
Volume I - Factor 1 - Technical Capability: Technical capability ratings reflect the Government's confidence in each offeror's product, as demonstrated in its submission, to meet the salient characteristics set forth in the solicitation. The Government will make an independent judgment of the acceptability. To receive consideration for award, a rating of no less than Acceptable must be achieved in technical acceptability.
(1) ACCEPTABLE - To receive this rating, the offeror's product must meet the salient characteristics set forth in the solicitation.
(2) UNACCEPTABLE - An unacceptable rating will be assessed on any offeror that demonstrates any of the following: Failure to meet the Salient Characteristics set forth in the solicitation. Failure to provide sufficient detail for the Government to determine whether the items satisfactorily meet the requirements of this solicitation.
Volume II - Factor 2 - Price:
Quotes will be reviewed to determine them fair and reasonable and if accepted by the Contracting Officer, will be incorporated into the resulting contract. Offeror's pricing must be determined to be fair and reasonable in order to be eligible for an award.

SECTION M - EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

In order to permit efficient competition and evaluation, the Navy may utilize the following methodology. Quotes will initially be assessed for price and ranked in order (lowest priced to highest priced). The Navy will then evaluate the technical factors of the lowest priced quote. In the event that this quote is deemed Unacceptable, the next lowest priced quote will be evaluated as many times as necessary, until the Government identifies a technically acceptable quote. The Navy will make award to the lowest priced technically acceptable quote, if possible. Accordingly, under this methodology, the technical factors of some quotes may not be evaluated by the Navy. The Government reserves the right to evaluate additional higher priced quoters to support a fair and reasonableness determination or for other considerations. If exchanges are deemed necessary by the Contracting Officer, a maximum of five (5) lowest priced quotes will be evaluated (both non-price and price) for the purpose of establishing a competitive range.

M-215-H004 BASIS OF AWARD (NAVSEA) (DEC 2018)

All units of all items will be awarded to one offeror. The resulting contract will be awarded to the low priced offeror who is deemed responsible in accordance with the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Part 9, as supplemented, whose proposal conforms to the solicitation's requirements and based on the evaluation factors is considered technically acceptable. Technical acceptability is determined by the offeror demonstrating the material/service being provided meets the solicitation requirements. The low offer will be analyzed to determine if the offeror's proposed price is fair and reasonable utilizing price analysis techniques in accordance with the guidelines in FAR Subpart 13.106-3.

Evaluation Process and Basis of Award
The purchase order resulting from this solicitation will be issued as a FIRM-FIXED PRICE purchase order. The purchase order will be made to the LOWEST-PRICE technically acceptable quote.
The USCG intends to award the purchase order based on the initial quote without engaging in exchanges. However, the USCG reserves the right to conduct exchanges with one or more of the vendors if determined by the Contracting Officer to be necessary and in the best interests of the USCG. The USCG may reject any or all quotes if such action is in the public interest and waive informalities and minor irregularities in quotes received. Vendors are strongly encouraged to submit their best quote in response to this solicitation.
The Government will first review the quotes for completeness/compliance (everything required to be submitted has been submitted). If a quote is found to be incomplete/non-compliant, it may be removed from the evaluation process, and it will not be further evaluated. Then the Government will list the remaining quotes by price, lowest to highest, and will begin the technical quote evaluation with the lowest priced Offeror. Upon finding the Offeror who meets the technical acceptability, the Offeror's price will be evaluated for reasonableness. If the first quote evaluated is technically acceptable with a price that is reasonable, evaluation will stop. If the first quote evaluated is either not technically acceptable or has a price that is not reasonable evaluation will continue. Once the lowest reasonably priced, technically acceptable quote is found evaluations will stop.

B. BASIS FOR AWARD (Section M)

The award will be made to a single responsible small business Offeror whose proposal is determined to be the lowest price, technically acceptable offer (FAR 15.101-2). To receive consideration for award, an "Acceptable" rating must be achieved for the Combined Technical/Management factor (to include all sub factors) and the Past Performance factor. The Cost/Price factor will not be rated, but will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness and for realism as specified in this solicitation. Any proposal receiving a rating of "Unacceptable" in any non-price Factor will not be eligible for award. The evaluation criteria detailing the Factors and Subfactors are contained in Section M of the solicitation and are herein incorporated by reference.

The Government reserves the right to award without discussions; therefore, offerors will be cautioned to ensure that their proposals contain all required information and are complete in ALL respects.

FACTORS AND SUB-FACTORS TO BE EVALUATED

The following evaluation factors and sub factors will be used to evaluate each proposal. Award will be made based on the lowest price of the proposal that is deemed technically acceptable.

A detailed explanation of the criteria for evaluation is set forth in the "Evaluation Approach," paragraph C of this section. During evaluation of each proposal, the Government will assign each factor/sub factor, excluding Price, an Acceptable/Unacceptable rating. Each sub factor will be evaluated to determine if the proposed approach associated with each sub factor meets or does not meet the minimum performance or capability requirements. To receive an overall "Acceptable" factor rating, all sub factors must receive an "Acceptable" rating.

1. INTRODUCTION: This requirement is a small business set aside under NAICS 541513, small business size standard of $37M. The solicitation shall provide the Government address and receipt date for proposal submittal. Proposals shall remain valid for a period of at least 90 days of the closing date of the solicitation.

Proposals must be submitted by electronic mail to the Contract Specialist, Kristena Burleigh at kristena.m.burleigh.civ@army.mil and copied to the Contracting Officer, Heather M. Caughorn, at heather.m.caughorn.civ@army.mil. It is the responsibility of the offeror to allow adequate time to submit proposal files before the submission deadline. Any proposal that is received by ACC-APG after the appointed deadline is "late" and may not be considered for award.

As this requirement is a small business set-aside, there are no subcontracting goals and a subcontracting plan is not required.

The offeror's proposal shall consist of four (4) volumes below. Each volume shall be contained in a separate electronic file. Proposals shall not contain classified data. The use of hyperlinks in proposals is prohibited.

VOLUME 1: FACTOR 1 - COMBINED TECHNICAL/MANAGEMENT

Sub-factor 1b: Staffing
Sub-factor 1c: Experience

VOLUME 3: FACTOR 3 - COST/PRICE
VOLUME 4: FACTOR 4 - REQUIRED DOCUMENTS

It is our understanding that Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection procedures are generally most appropriate for the acquisition of non-complex services with minimal performance risk. Section 813 of the FY17 NDAA, as amended by Section 822 of the FY18 NDAA, encourages DoD contracting officers to avoid, to the maximum extent practicable, the use of LPTA for procurements that are predominately for IT services, systems engineering and technical assistance, cybersecurity services, or other knowledge-based professional services. Given that the scope of this requirement includes complex, knowledge-based ERP, and IT functional support services as described in the PWS, would the Government consider the use of a Best Value Tradeoff source selection methodology in lieu of LPTA for this acquisition?

For this acquisition, the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) will be utilized as stated in the solicitation.

(c) Contractors shall identify in their proposal a company point of contact (POC) for oral proposals by providing the POC's name, phone number, and email address. Menlo Security, Inc. is excluded from participating in any oral presentations. The Government will schedule oral proposals with the point of contact for contractors who submit a complete package by the due date/time specified within the table in paragraph 1. The Government intends to schedule oral proposals approximately 3-5 working days after submission of the written proposal and slides. Dates/times will be assigned at random for all contractors.

(d) Representation at the oral proposal is limited to four individuals per contractor. The four individuals shall consist of three speakers/presenters and one silent party driving the presentation. The three individuals that are speakers/presenters must include personnel who are being proposed to work on the effort and at least one individual must be either the individual who will be identified as key personnel upon award or proposing company's executive. Each company and individual may only attend one oral proposal, either for itself as a prime contractor, or as a subcontractor for another contractor.

(e) The oral proposals will be held virtually via a secure platform using Microsoft Teams. Contractors will not be required to have a Common Access Card (CAC) in order to access the platform for the briefing. The Government will be recording the briefings. All expenses accrued relating to oral proposals shall be paid by the contractor; no travel or other costs will be reimbursed by the Government.

(g) The oral proposals and the slides as well as the price volume will be used in the LPTA determination. However, Pricing Information shall only be included in the Price Volume, and not part of the Technical/Management Volume or Oral Proposal.

a. Basis for Contract Award
Award will be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is determined to represent the overall best value to the Government using a lowest price technically acceptable (LPTA) evaluation process. The Government anticipates awarding to the lowest price technically acceptable respondent from initial contractor responses, without engaging in exchanges with respondents. Respondents are strongly encouraged to submit their best technical solution and price/cost in response to this solicitation.

The technical proposal should include all information the offeror wants the Government to consider and evaluate regarding its ability to perform all work described in the evaluation subfactors. To be eligible for award, the offeror's proposal must sufficiently address all aspects of the evaluation factors and subfactors. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the offeror being ineligible for award.

Section 508 Accessibility Standards do apply to this requirement. Section 508 requirements are listed in the attached PWS (Attachment 1). The offeror should notify the Government in its proposal if any exception to Section 508 standards is proposed. Any proposal failing to meet the Section 508 Accessibility Standards, as listed in the PWS, may be eliminated from consideration for award.

E.11 BASIS FOR AWARD

Award will be made to the lowest priced, responsive, responsible offeror whose quote conforms to the requirements of the solicitation set forth in the Price Schedule and Product Description. The total evaluated price shall be the sum of all proposed CLIN(s)/SLIN(s) set forth in Section B.4 Price Schedule of the RFQ.

All offerors are advised that, in the interest of efficiency, the Government reserves the right to conduct the evaluation in the most effective manner. Specifically, the Government may first evaluate the total proposed price of all offerors. Thereafter, the Government will evaluate the quote of the lowest priced offeror only. If the lowest priced offeror's quote is determined to conform to the requirements, the Government may make award to that offeror without further evaluation of the remaining offerors' quotes. If it is determined that the lowest priced offeror's quote does not conform to the requirement, then the Government may evaluate the next lowest priced quote and so forth and so on, until the Government reaches the lowest priced quote that is determined to conform to the requirement. However, the Government reserves the right to evaluate all offerors' quotes should it desire to conduct discussions or otherwise determine it to be in the Government's best interest.

Section M - Evaluation Factors for Award
52.212-2 ADDENDUM
BASIS FOR AWARD AND EVALUATION CRITERIA
This acquisition will be solicited, evaluated and awarded using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable, Source Selection, as supplemented by the Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS), and the Army Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (AFARS). A contract will be awarded to the Offeror deemed responsible in accordance with the FAR, as supplemented, whose proposal conforms to the solicitation's requirements and is judged the lowest price technically acceptable to the Government.
As a basis for award, trade-offs between price and non-price factors are not permitted. Offers received for less than the stated requirement will be considered ineligible for award. As set forth in FAR 52.215-1(f)(1), the Government intends to award a contract resulting from this solicitation to the responsible Offeror whose proposal represents the lowest price technically acceptable. Therefore, the Offeror's initial proposal should contain the Offeror's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. IAW FAR 15.408-2, discussions may be held. However, the Government may choose to award a contract without discussions. If discussions are determined unnecessary and award is made from initial proposals, proposals rated as 'Unacceptable' in Evaluation Factors 1 and/or 2 will not be considered for award.

IV. EVALUATION

1. EVALUATION PROCESS:

a. At the time of bid review, each will be checked for the presence or absence of required information in conformance with the submission requirements of this RFQ.

b. The State will evaluate each quote to determine its responsiveness to the published requirements.

c. Award, if made, will be to the responsive and responsible bidder with the lowest total cost, after application of any applicable incentives and preferences.

III. RESPONSIBLE BIDDER

This RFQ will be awarded to the lowest, responsive, responsible Bidder. CDT may require Bidder(s) to submit evidence of their qualifications at such times and under conditions, as it may deem necessary. If a Bidder has been determined to be non-responsible, the RFQ response shall be rejected.

The Bid may be rejected if it is conditional or incomplete, or if it contains any alterations of form or other irregularities of any kind. CDT may reject any or all Bids and may waive any immaterial deviation in the Bid. CDT's waiver of an immaterial defect shall in no way modify the Bid document or excuse the Bidder from full compliance with all the requirements if awarded the Agreement.

The Federal Bureau of Prisons (BOP) will utilize the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR Part 12. The Government intends to make a firm-fixed-unit price IDIQ award to the responsible business whose quotation provides the lowest price technically acceptable to the Government.
Proposals will be evaluated for technical acceptability based on the factors and sub-factors set forth below. No tradeoffs between price and non-price factors are permitted. A proposal must receive an "Acceptable" rating in every non-price factor to be eligible for award.
Definitions: For purposes of better understanding the decisions made based upon evaluation criteria selected for the Accreditation Services Contract for FY2026-2030 the evaluation terms for Technical have been defined as follows:
1.0 Technical Acceptability Definitions
For the purposes of this evaluation, the following definitions apply to all Technical Factors:
Acceptable: The proposal meets the minimum requirements of the solicitation and the Statement of Work.
Unacceptable: The proposal fails to meet the minimum requirements of the solicitation or contains deficiencies that increase the risk of unsuccessful contract performance to an unacceptable level.
2.2 Price Evaluation
Price will be the deciding factor among all proposals rated as "Technically Acceptable." The Government will evaluate price for:
1. Total Evaluated Price: Inclusion of the base period and all option years.
2. Reasonableness: Comparison of quotes to ensure the price is fair and reasonable.
3. Realism: Review to ensure the proposed price is not so low as to indicate a risk of performance failure.
Basis for Award: The Government intends to award a contract to the responsible offeror whose proposal is the lowest evaluated price and is technically Acceptable.

1.0 Basis for Contract Award:
This acquisition will be conducted under the procedures of FAR Part 12 -- Commercial Items and FAR Part 13 -- Simplified Acquisition Procedures. This acquisition will utilize price as the only evaluated factor. Award will be made to the vendor with the lowest total evaluated price (TEP) and whose quotation conforms to the solicitation requirements (to include all stated terms, conditions, representations, and certifications). The Government intends to award without interchanges. However, the Government reserves the right to conduct interchanges if determined necessary by the Contracting Officer (ref. FAR 52.212-2 Addendum, section 1.3).

1.1 Solicitation Requirements (Terms and Conditions)
Vendors are required to meet all solicitation requirements, such as terms and conditions, representations and certifications, and items list requirements, in addition to the price factor. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the vendor being ineligible for award. Vendors must clearly identify any exception to the solicitation terms and conditions and must provide complete supporting rationale. The Government reserves the right to determine any such exceptions unacceptable, and the quotation, therefore, ineligible for award.

BACKGROUND: A facility in Tulsa, Oklahoma, is currently being renovated for the purpose of being used as the new Tulsa VA Medical Center (VAMC) in June 2026. The facility will need an Elopement Alarm System between May 2026 and August 2026. The facility is currently under control of VHiT and the construction contractor remodeling the facility. The Elopement Alarm System is required to be installed during the hospital activation period so that it is functional before first patient is seen.

LOCATION: The new Tulsa VA Medical Center will be located at 440 S. Houston Ave, Tulsa, Oklahoma 74127.

PERFORMANCE PERIOD/DELIVERY TIMES: The VA requires delivery and installation of product between May 2026 and August 2026 as required by the construction contractor. After award delivery times will be coordinated through the COR.

The term of this contract will be from September 1, 2026, through August 31, 2027, with two possible one-year renewals. VALUE OF CONTRACT

There is an urgent need to award a follow-on task order by March 30, 2026, to ensure uninterrupted professional staffing support for ASPR's Center for Industrial Base Management and Supply Chain (IBMSC) Defense Production Act (DPA) – Emergency Response Authorities (ERA) Office DPA Title III (industrial base expansion) Program. Due to a series of unforeseen federal and Departmental actions, a new competitive professional services staffing contract/task order was not awarded as originally planned. This justification supports a limited source, bridge GSA task order in accordance with GSAR 538.7104-3(b)(1)(i). An unusual urgent and compelling need exists to continue services as a severe acquisition delay was caused by the shutdown. Failure to award a contract action quickly will cause a disruption of service and a significant negative impact to Regional 3 business operations. LinTech Global, Inc. currently provides IT support services to EPA Regions 1, 2 and 3 under the current GSA task order and therefore is fully capable of continuing to perform these services without any break in service. The bridge would cover a three (3) month base period of performance beginning from date of award and would include one (1) 3-month option period (if needed thereafter). The total period of performance for the bridge is not expected to exceed six (6) months in total duration to include the option periods. The period of performance is expected to cover the timeframe until the requirement can be properly solicited, evaluated and awarded. REQUIREMENT DESCRIPTION:
Minimum Viable Product Requirements:
NCIS is seeking to conduct a proof of concept for a minimally viable product (MVP) to show a proof of concept for future CMS development. The Government intends to award multiple contracts in a phased approach; an initial contract for the proof-of-concept and a follow-on contract for the implementation of the enterprise-wide CMS. The base period of the initial contract will consist of a two (2) month proof of concept period to support functional and technical review of the Offeror's solution. The focus for this initial phase will be to provide a 'new' user interface (UI) that works concurrently with NCIS' existing data structure. Users will have the ability to use both the existing UI and the 'new' UI during the contract period.

The proposed contract will provide services to operate and maintain the MEGS for the Navy Medical Center San Diego (NMCSD) within the NAVFAC SW area of responsibility (AOR). The period of performance for the proposed contract is an extension for 6-months. The MEGS is an Iconics Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition System (SCADA). MEGS provides a control system that continuously monitors the operation and performance of the NMCSD Co-Generation (COGEN) utility system. The COGEN system provides continuous, reliable, on-site utilities (electrical power, water, steam, compressed air) for the NMCSD. The system includes generation units, control systems, automatic transfer switching, emissions and compliance equipment, and integration with NMCSD's existing electrical infrastructure. Estimated Dollar Value: The estimated dollar value of this contract action is approximately $255,000.00 based on the Independent Government Estimate (IGE), utilizing historical data, input from subject matter experts, and market research. 4.3.1. The white paper shall address, at a minimum, the following:
a) Short compliance statement, confirming that the proposals comply with the RFI scope, meaning that they:
   a. Are not based on the demonstration of a commercial product.
   b. Are not cyber security focused.
   c. Contribute to (and support) cyberspace operations, from a military standpoint.
   d. Are affordable in terms of timeline and resources (see below ROM).
   e. Do not require large training datasets nor access to actual classified information.
b) Experiment proposal(s) formulated in terms of objectives, hypotheses, success criteria, technical set up, metrics, etc. Experiments should seek to provide a meaningful deliverable/product upon completion.
c) Brief description (maturity, use cases, etc.) of the concept, product or capability to be experimented with.
d) Effort/cost (ROM) required to conduct the proposed experiment.
 To help guide respondents, HQ SACT wishes to highlight that first-phase experiments are usually modest in scale, often completed within about six months and designed to operate within a limited level of effort.

5.2 The Vendor shall provide each attendee with a password to take both Scrum Master and Product Owner Credentialing exams.

The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

● The vendor shall utilize Agile delivery standards including:
● Crossfunctional teams with rapid iteration and regular testing
● Sprint velocity management through historical data analysis

Software development services shall, to the maximum extent possible, follow the established NGA Agile software development processes and procedures. This includes an Agile requirements management system using the current Scaled Agile Framework for all work performed, and the integration of Contractor development requirements (story and features) with the higher-level government requirements system (Program Epics and Solution Epics in USMS Jira on COE). Software maintenance services shall, to the maximum extent possible, follow the established NGA Agile software maintenance processes and procedures. Software documentation and testing data (plans, procedures, and results) shall utilize modern digital engineering tools as opposed to formal documents whenever possible.

Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI)). The nature of this action being approved is to bridge contract to the Agile Web Presence II (AWP II) Firm-Fixed Price (FFP) contract, GSA Number 47QTCA19D00CY, Contract number HM0476-20-F-0193, to extend the period of performance and increase the contract value. The scope for the AWP-II contract was determined to be necessary to support national security and to protect life and property, both in the continental United States and overseas. The current contract is set to expire on 29 March 2026 and an extension of the current contract is required to serve as a bridge until a follow-on competitive contract can be awarded. The AWP-II program provides continued operations and sustainment to the AWP web-based capability known as 'The Globe', an enterprise-wide, centralized web presence for discovery of NGA products, services, and tools, m.Globe, and Discovery Catalog on the Unclassified, NIPR, SIPR, JWICS, CAPNET, and Stoneghost domains within the IC. 'The Globe' collectively supports 178,000+ unique users across multiple security domains in their discovery of mission-critical geospatial intelligence for the Department of War (DoW), Intelligence Community (IC), National System for Geospatial-Intelligence (NSG), and Allied System for Geospatial-Intelligence (ASG) through the integration of geospatial programs and services. In accordance with FAR 8.405-6(a)(1)(i)(C), the required scope to the AWPII contract represents a logical follow-on to the original order in the interest of economy and efficiency. This requirement will have a Period of Performance (POP) that consistent of a 12-month Base Period and two (2) 6-month Optional Periods. Total POP will be for 24 months.

3.2 (U) Software Development
(U) The Contractor shall provide software development services to ensure the definition, design, implementation, and testing of the software to meet the functional and performance of new or augmented mission requirements while maximizing the effectiveness and efficiency. These new capabilities might include new sensor data integration or a new capability transitioned at "Initial Operational Capability (IOC)" that requires some development to reach "Full Operational Capability (FOC)" capability maturity. These services include the following:
• Sprint/Release planning
• Software coding
• Software integration
• Software unit testing and reporting (CDRL-A009, CDRL-A010)
• Software deployment to operations (CDRL-A008)
• Software enterprise/user testing

(U) Software development services shall, to the maximum extent possible, follow the established NGA Agile software development processes and procedures. This includes an Agile requirements management system using the current Scaled Agile Framework for all work performed, and the integration of Contractor development requirements (story and features) with the higher-level government requirements system (Program Epics and Solution Epics in USMS Jira on COE). The Contractor shall maintain an USMS Jira project on COE as the authoritative source of truth for all system work, ensuring that Features reflect current information and status.

6.2.1 Subtask 1 – Product Backlog
The contractor shall develop and deliver an agile Product Backlog that is organized in a hierarchy where Epics are decomposed into Features and Features are decomposed into User Stories with bi-directional traceability between each level.

The contractor shall collaborate with key stakeholders, including the Government Product Owner to elicit, document, refine, and prioritize backlog items. Backlog items shall contain, at a minimum, the requisite information:
• Title
• Description
• Level of Effort (e.g., story points, hours, etc.)
• Definition of Done / Acceptance Criteria
• Dependencies
• Other attributes that characterize the story

Stories shall fall into one of four states:
• Proposed: The story is complete (contains the required attributes) but has not been approved by the Government Product Owner.
• Approved: The story has been approved, and work can begin.
• In-Progress: The story is approved, assigned to a team, and is currently under development.
• Done: The story has been completed (meeting the definition of done and all acceptance criteria) and is accepted by the Government Product Owner.

The contractor shall employ agile backlog grooming practices, including backlog refinement sessions, to continuously review, update, and reprioritize items based on stakeholder feedback, technical feasibility, and business value. The backlog shall be maintained in an agile project management tool designated by the Government.

2.3.1 The Contractor shall perform software maintenance based upon priorities established by the Government in accordance with AGILE principles and in accordance with the Agile Development Management Plan (ADMP) (CDRL AXXX).

a) Corrective – System changes to correct error and operational degradation.
• Troubleshooting and fixing minor application bugs, including system restarts.
• Reconfiguring Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) for specific user / use case errors.
• Working with ISP, CSP and other system admins to resolve Discrepancy Reports (DRs) caused by external factors that arise in non-AWP capabilities.
• Fast, one-time fixes (aka "hot fixes") that do not involve agile software development, backlog creation, or using the more formal development and sustainment processes described in other service definitions.
• Restoring application or system services that are down.
b) Preventative - System changes to prevent degradation of service.
c) Adaptive – System changes to address changing operational environments
• Modify/enhance software code, including UX/UI, baseline in support of new/emerging software/system requirements in accordance with the Agile Development Management Plan (CDRL A003).
d) Perfective – System changes to optimize operations and performance and comply with relevant policy and directive.
• Modify/enhance software code baseline, including UX/UI, in support of new/emerging software/system requirements and design changes in accordance with the Agile Development Management Plan (CDRL A003).

THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY - EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

SOFTWARE METRICS
To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster and more consistently. To measure progress toward this intent, NGA will track three key metrics for each software product: availability, lead time for changes, and deployment frequency. Additionally, each software product is expected to have its own product-specific metrics to track how well the product is working for its users. Together, these metrics balance speed and reliability and describe a product's impact and value.

The metrics and elements in this document, collectively referred to as "The NGA Software Way," serve as an implementation guide to supplement NGA's Technology Strategy and provide additional specific guidance for teams building and operating software.

For software teams using NGA's enterprise-managed version control and Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipeline, deployment frequency and lead time for changes will be automatically tracked within those services' dashboards. Teams must prioritize rapidly getting a product to actual users in production to learn what works and how to successfully iterate; this first capability must be delivered in months, not years. DORA research shows high-performing teams deploy at least once per week.

3. Prioritize Rapidly Delivering Value to Users
Once user needs are deeply understood, the team must decide which of those needs to prioritize. To do this, the team must define a minimum viable product (MVP), either in written form or, ideally, as a set of wireframes, and then share it with key stakeholders, customers, and users. The MVP is the smallest actual deliverable that can be reviewed by real customers to get feedback on if it solves a need and to test assumptions, learn quickly, and decrease risk upfront. The product manager must break down the MVP into actionable work items that are easily understood by the entire team. Teams must gather feedback as early as possible using mockups or interactive prototypes. As feedback is gathered, the prototype's fidelity should mature, and the team must transition to testing with the real product.

Each day a product isn't actively solving user needs in production increases the risk the team is building the wrong thing. Teams must prioritize rapidly getting a product to actual users in production to learn what works and how to successfully iterate; this first capability must be delivered in months, not years. If the initially defined MVP cannot be delivered to real users in production within single-digit months, it must be broken down further.

13. Iterate and Improve Continually
Products and services are never "finished." Getting real people using your product as early as possible, monitoring the product's performance, and making improvements throughout the lifetime of the product enable teams to adapt quickly. Making improvements means more than doing basic maintenance like fixing defects in code, deploying security patches, and keeping runbooks up to date; continual improvement means you can capture and respond to changes in user needs, technology, or government policy throughout the lifetime of the product.
Iteration isn't just for the early stages of a product's development; a team must be able to make substantial improvements throughout a product's lifetime. As product complexity grows, teams must focus on balancing paying down technical debt with new feature development, ensuring the product continues to be adaptable over time to respond rapidly to user needs.
Questions to Answer
► How has your product evolved in the past six months? ► What is your usability testing process and cadence?
► What are the biggest upcoming changes, and why are ► What changes have you made to the product based on they needed? usability testing?
► Can you share your product roadmap?
CONCLUSION
The above metrics and 13 elements, collectively referred to as "The NGA Software Way," ensure each NGA software product has the necessary people, processes, and tools in place to successfully deliver. Together, they enable NGA to consistently build, release, and operate software that meets our users' needs and expectations and deliver on our mission.

A NOTE FROM THE CTO

To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster and more consistently. To do this, the NGA Technology Strategy describes changes in approach, tools, and behavior that will result in faster iterations, more flexible products, and better-satisfied users and customers. The NGA Software Way is an implementation guide to supplement NGA's Technology Strategy and provide additional specific guidance for teams building and operating software.

Our intent is to get to a place where NGA software products are designed and built with users, can be automatically updated in production daily, and use product-specific metrics to make decisions. We believe in small, empowered, cross-functional teams that deeply understand their users and constantly work directly with those users to deliver on their mission.

Implementing The NGA Software Way won't happen all at once; we must start with iteratively tracking and improving these metrics and meeting each element below. As we do this together, and with an understanding of this intent, we will successfully deliver software products with the speed, accuracy, and precision NGA's mission demands.

The three initial metrics all programs must track for each software product delivered to users are availability, lead time for changes, and deployment frequency. It is important to note that NGA is not unique in using these metrics. These are three of the primary metrics DevOps Research and Assessment (DORA) has studied over the past six years. These metrics provide visibility into how NGA balances speed (deployment frequency and lead time for changes) and stability (availability) during product development and operations.

2. Deeply Understand User Needs
Before beginning technical work, a product manager and team must engage with real and/or expected users to identify and understand users' underlying needs, as well as learn how the product or service will fit into the users' work. This is not just a government product manager (or anyone else) hearing users say what they "want." It requires deeply understanding their mission and goals as well as current approaches. Only by understanding the existing state and the reasoning behind it can the team design and engineer a solution.

Human-centered design involves engaging with users to understand their problems and concerns. Methods for doing so include interviews, usability testing, and prototyping. The identified user needs, including KPIs and key findings, must be regularly shared with stakeholders and kept in NGA's enterprise-managed collaboration tools.

3. Prioritize Rapidly Delivering Value to Users
Teams must define a minimum viable product (MVP), either in written form or, ideally, as a set of wireframes, and then share it with key stakeholders, customers, and users. The MVP is the smallest actual deliverable that can be reviewed by real customers to get feedback on if it solves a need and to test assumptions, learn quickly, and decrease risk upfront. Teams must gather feedback as early as possible using mockups or interactive prototypes. As feedback is gathered, the prototype's fidelity should mature, and the team must transition to testing with the real product. Market research must be conducted to understand if a solution already exists inside or outside of NGA that sufficiently meets user needs or can be easily altered to do so. Only after thorough analysis of the existing landscape can software product teams confidently determine whether to buy, augment, or build a solution to address user needs.

VETERAN FOCUSED INTEGRATION PROCESS (VIP) AND PRODUCT LINE MANAGEMENT (PLM)

The Contractor shall support VA efforts IAW the updated Veteran-Focused Integration Process (VIP) and Product Line Management (PLM) Playbook. The major focus of VIP is on Enterprise Oversight, Governance, reporting, and establishing practices that focus on outcomes and continuously delivering sustainable IT capabilities to benefit the Veteran. PLM is a framework that focuses on delivering functional products that provide the highest priority work to customers while delivering simplified, reliable, and practical solutions to the business, medical staff, and our Veterans. The VIP Guide is a companion guide to the PLM Transformation Playbook and can be found at: https://www.voa.va.gov/DocumentView.aspx?DocumentID=4371 and the PLM Transformation Playbook can be found at https://www.voa.va.gov/DocumentView.aspx?DocumentID=4946. VIP and PLM are the authoritative processes that IT projects must follow to ensure development and delivery of IT products.

The PLM Transformation Playbook details how OIT leadership expects Product Lines (PLs) to implement PLM, DevOps, and Lean-Agile principles, methods, practices, and techniques through levels of maturity. The PLM Transformation Playbook applies to all Information Technology (IT) products aligned to PLs in the Software Product Management, Infrastructure Operations and Product Engineering organizations within the OIT organization.

The primary objective of this award is to continue to provide support, maintenance, and enhancements to the TruData Modernization Data Platform. TruData Modernization will become the sole data platform that will form the backbone of FMCSA's IT modernization. FMCSA CTO, in collaboration with OCIO and other FMCSA leaders has set a strategy to build a Data Platform that will include a Data Lake, Data Warehouses and Data Marts that will enable efficient data storage, exchange and analysis for improving the registration, inspection, compliance and enforcement mission functions for FMCSA. The TruData Modernization effort will also streamline data exchanges between mission systems and eliminate redundant capabilities delivered by SAFER, SAFETYNET and other data structures implemented solely for the purpose of information sharing between systems. A continued innovative technical approach is needed to fully implement the data platform for FMCSA and realize the objectives outlined in TruData Modernization Bridge SOO. Specifically: The Contractor shall provide non-personal, IT systems support, staffing controls, program management, and shall deliver services both on and off-site as necessary to execute against all requirements established in the SOO and as set forth by the Government. All development and testing will occur within DOT/FMCSA environments, or instances of environments licensed to DOT/FMCSA, unless explicitly permitted by the COR in writing. Continue the development process with the entire contractor and government team that: develops end-to-end capability in an agile manner; facilitates open communication through regular check-ins and collaborative sessions; and provides clear actors/activities/documents/outcomes.
