Decision to Use the RFP Process
Pursuant to Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 30B, Section 6, the Town of Easton is utilizing a Request for Proposals (RFP) process for the procurement of an Integrated Financial Accounting Software System.

This procurement involves complex and integrated services that cannot be evaluated solely on the basis of price. The system must support governmental fund accounting, comply with applicable GASB and regulatory requirements, integrate with existing municipal systems, ensure data security, and provide implementation, training, and ongoing technical support.

Selection of a vendor requires evaluation of qualitative factors including technical capabilities, system functionality, vendor experience, implementation approach, cybersecurity safeguards, references, and long-term support capacity. These criteria necessitate a comparative evaluation process to determine the most advantageous proposal, taking into consideration both price and non-price factors.

Accordingly, the RFP process under Chapter 30B, Section 6 is appropriate and in the best interest of the municipality to ensure a fair, competitive, and value-based procurement.

The Town intends to select the most advantageous proposal, taking into consideration technical merit, experience, and price.

PURPOSE
The purpose of this Request for Proposals is to solicit interest from qualified firms to provide an inmate management system to the detention center.

INMATE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM SPECIFICATIONS
The Campbell County Detention Center, referred to as "CCDC", is requesting proposals from providers of inmate identification, security rounds, and activity logging. CCDC is particularly interested in proposals that include the use of technology in automating key operational workflows, tasks, and correctional reporting requirements as mandated by state corrections standards. This includes, but not limited to, tracking inmate movements and activities, logging cell checks, security checks, headcounts, inmate facial recognition software, commissary pass and other logs generated by CCDC for the purpose of tracking inmate interaction.

The Inmate Management System must be able to track inmate movements via passive, electronic means and logging of cell checks and security checks via fixed electronic scannable tags. All electronic log entries must record inmate name, inmate jacket number, deputy identification, date and time of entries. Electronically captured data cannot be edited, deleted, or altered in any way.

The Inmate Management System must: Interface with Jail Tracker, CCDC's existing jail management system; Use of a ruggedized handheld device with capabilities of sustaining a minimum of a 12 hour shift of average usage before recharging battery and have the ability to retrieve reports of inmate activity.

CCDC houses a daily population range of 400 to 500 inmates and maximum population of approximately 656 inmates.

APPENDIX B – SECTION M: Evaluation Criteria

Proposals will be evaluated on multiple factors to ensure the best overall value for the government. As part of this assessment, the government will consider the offeror's depth and relevance of experience with Microsoft Defender and Microsoft Purview in a government M365 E5/G5 environment. This experience will be evaluated in conjunction with other technical, management, and performance considerations and will not serve as the sole basis for determination:
• Demonstrated success in securing PII and sensitive agency information
• Ability to deploy, configure, and tune Purview DLP across workloads
• Proficiency in threat detection, incident response, and endpoint protection
• Understanding of GCC-specific considerations and limitations

Higher ratings where candidates demonstrate advanced certifications, extensive hands-on experience, or documented success implementing multi-layered security architectures using Defender, Purview, and complementary tools.

D.2 Evaluator Scoring Rubric (Illustrative Weights)
Criterion Weight Indicators of Strength
Purview DLP across workloads 25% — Intent→config mapping; SITs+EDM; monthly KPIs; exfil tests; tuning cycles
Defender stack + Sentinel 20% — Runbooks, triage workflows, SIEM integration use cases
Insider Risk & Adaptive Protection 15% — IRM policies; Adaptive Protection scoping; rollout notes
GCC/GCC High/DoD awareness & licensing 15% — Clear feature differences, compliance handling for CUI/PII
Email exfil protection 10% — Subject/body/attachments; domain scoping; Policy Tips/wait-on-send
Endpoint & Browser/Domain controls 10% — Copy/paste/print/USB restrictions; sensitive domain groups; approved browsers
Documentation & Knowledge Transfer 5% — Playbooks, naming conventions, exception handling, user education

Performance Work Statement
AFLCMC Engineering Directorate (EN-EZ)
Collateral Conference Room 162
1.0 Background
The U.S. Government requires a technology upgrade for the conference room located at conference room 162 located in Building 28, Area B, WPAFB, OH The purpose is to integrate a modern Unified Communications (UC) capability using Microsoft Teams into a Collateral Secret SIPRNET environment. The room contains existing VTC equipment that is outdated and scheduled for decommissioning but will remain in place temporarily. The new solution must be integrated with certain remaining components while replacing those that are incompatible.
2.0 Scope
The contractor shall provide and install a complete, turn-key solution to enable MS Teams functionality within the designated conference room. The work includes providing and installing a new camera, microphone system, and control system processor, and integrating these components with the existing displays and control interface. The final system must be capable of dual operation, allowing users to select either the new MS Teams capability or the legacy VTC system until it is decommissioned. The solution must be engineered to function independently after the legacy system is removed without requiring rework.

3.1 BACKGROUND
Morgan State University operates independently and is not linked to any human resources firms, subsidiary entities, or external consultants in the procurement of this software.
Morgan State University seeks to modernize its human resources capabilities by implementing a comprehensive Human Resources Management System (HRMS). The University aims to adopt a unified HRMS that supports the full spectrum of HR activities, including recruitment, onboarding, performance management, compensation management, learning management, employee relations case management, workforce planning, document management, compliance reporting, employee self-service functionalities, and manager self-service functionalities.
There will be approximately 25 HR team members who will require elevated access to manage their functions. The university has approximately 2400 total employees who would be customer end users.
The university plans to award a single award encompassing both the software and its implementation services; separate sourcing for implementation services will not be used. The procurement specialist will need to secure multiple approvals prior to finalizing a contract award, including necessary endorsements from internal stakeholders and, where pertinent, the Board of Public Works (BPW) if the contract value surpasses a defined threshold.

FINANCIAL SOFTWARE INTEGRATED WITH HUMAN RESOURCES AND PAYROLL
Specifications
The system/programs shall be fully integrated and meet the requirements established and in full compliance as an enterprise resource planning system (ERP) in accordance with NJ.A.C. 6A:23A-6.7. The system/programs must be user friendly and intuitive, eliminate or reduce existing manual processes, produce reports in compliance with requirements of the State of New Jersey Department of Education and ad hoc queries quickly and easily. The system/programs must provide secure access to proprietary information in accordance with permissions granted, be scalable and customizable.

a. The application must be cloud based.
b. Provider's software must have been in use ten plus years in various size New Jersey school districts.
c. Application must run on both PC's and Mac's.
d. Data transmissions must be encrypted utilizing SSL.
e. The system should be scalable.
f. The system/programs must include document imaging links and ability to drill down for detail on any previewed report.
g. The programs must include the option for electronic approvals and electronic check signatures, and the proposal shall stipulate the internal security established by the system. An accredited and nationally recognized e-signature authority/provider should be used and identified.

SECTION M - EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1 Technical Evaluation Criteria

The following criteria shall be utilized in evaluation of the technical quote. Each of the following criteria will be evaluated on a Pass/Fail basis in accordance with the criteria descriptions included in Section M. In order to be eligible for award, your quote should include adequate detail and information for the Government evaluators to be able to rate each criteria as satisfactory (Pass). A rating of "Fail" in any one of these three criteria will render the quote ineligible for award until the deficiencies are corrected.

Technical evaluations for this RFQ are as follows: (limited to 20 pages)

1. Technical Capability:
• The Offeror shall provide a detailed and comprehensive statement of their capability to provide license access to a digital evaluation and analysis platform for the purposes of conducting several types of rapid online user tests remotely on both desktop and mobile platforms, and includes a combination of unmoderated short user tests, unmoderated standard user tests and moderated user tests with live participants recruited from the platforms' Contributor Network of public health professionals, and the ability to capture user screens and comments as participants complete evaluation activities without video recording participants or other personally identifiable information. The Offeror's explanation shall include name of the digital evaluation platform and an analysis of the platform task requirements to indicate their understanding of the requirement.

2. Management Approach:
• The Offeror shall provide a detailed and comprehensive approach to the problem, scope, and purpose of the project to demonstrate a complete understanding of the intent, operating environment, and project objectives of the contract.

The above listed criteria are listed in order of descending importance and, when combined, the technical factors are significantly more important than price.

2. Scope of Work.
The scope of work includes the procurement, implementation, training and support of an advanced weapons detection system designed to enhance campus safety through real-time video analytics and seamless integration with the existing security infrastructure.

1. Accuracy
a. Accurately identifying a weapon when it is present. Leading systems often achieve high accuracy rates, such as 95–99%, for clearly visible weapons.
b. Minimizing false alarms when an object is not a weapon.
c. Availability of visible confidence level shown for each alarm
d. Reliable long-range operation, detecting weapons from a distance, dependent on camera resolution.
2. Speed
a. Low Latency- processing video almost instantaneously
b. High frame rate- to avoid missing threats
3. Robustness- perform reliably under challenging conditions
a. Environmental resilience- various lighting conditions low-light, shadows and weather
b. Visual Clutter- campus atmosphere heavy traffic at times
4. Weapon recognition capability- The system should be able to identify a wide range of weapons, including handguns, rifles, shotguns, and knives.
5. Integration with existing systems
a. Camera compatibility: Working with standard Internet Protocol (IP) cameras of multiple manufacturers and Genetec (VMS).
b. Multi-camera support: Monitoring and analyzing feeds from an entire network of cameras.

2.4 HOTLINE SUPPORT AND PERFORMANCE MONITORING

Functionality and accuracy of the installed equipment will be continuously monitored by VA Logistics staff to ensure equipment operates according to the salient characteristics and provides accurate and timely information for inventory and reordering purposes. VA will notify the Contractor's Support Hotline of any issues via Contractor's support phone number or email.

The Contractor shall provide VA with a Support Hotline telephone number and email that VA can use to notify the Contractor of any issues with the installed equipment or software. The Support Hotline shall be staffed 7:00AM – 8:00PM EST and available 24x7x365 to provide support or assistance to VA. The Contractor shall respond to service calls no later than 24 hours after being notified of a problem with either equipment or installed software and resolve within 48 hours of notification by VA, any defects or functionality problems detected.

The Contractor shall work with VA onsite to replace supported hardware within 48 hours of notification of a problem if VA is not at fault for damage or defect. This assumes there is backup equipment onsite. If backup equipment is not onsite, the Contractor's support staff shall work with VA onsite to replace equipment when it arrives via Contractor's return merchandise authorization process.

The Contractor shall provide performance monitoring. The Contractor shall monitor the installed equipment and software remotely by daily check-ins that shall occur remotely at least four times every 24 hours for 12 months for each period of performance after installation completed. The Contractor shall notify VA and provide maintenance support in the event of a problem with equipment or software detected during the performance monitoring.

Project Methodology: Structure and follow a project management approach for planning, organizing, and managing the staff and activities throughout the life of the project. The Bidder's project management approach must facilitate open and timely communication with the Office and a strong working relationship to achieve the overall goal of completed system design performance that meets or exceeds user needs on time and within budget.

Change Management: Formally document and track all changes to the functional design, technical design, and/or to approved deliverables. For each potential change, the Project Manager will work closely with the Office's Project Manager to define and document the change, its benefits, and if necessary, its impact to the project schedule and budget. No work associated with a documented change will begin without first receiving written authorization from the Office's Project Manager.

Workplan: The Project Manager will be required to maintain a detailed project work plan, with content to be approved by the Office's Project Manager, through the full term of the contract. The Bidder must provide a detailed project-management plan outlining major tasks, milestones, and dependencies.

The project work plan will be created and maintained with an automated project management tool (e.g., Microsoft Project) and will include appropriate detail to provide project status, dependencies, and risk/mitigation.

The project work plan must include: Design and Discovery Phase to refine workflows, confirm data sources, and validate user requirements. Identify milestones for prototype demonstrations, stakeholder reviews, and phased releases. Coordinate parallel development of both platforms to ensure interoperability.

Offerors must propose the following key personnel positions for the following eFAST Labor Categories with the following responsibilities:

Program Manager
Security Assessment Lead
Penetration Testing Lead
NCO Technical Lead

The Security Assessment Lead must have within the required years of experience listed in the labor category, five years of management and supervisory experience, responsible for the successful completion of multiple risk and vulnerability tasks.

The Penetration Testing Lead is responsible for leading all penetration testing efforts under the contract. They serve as the primary Point of Contact between the Government's Program Office, COR and CO for all penetration testing and issues. They must attend all Program Management Reviews (PMRs) with the Program Manager and report the status of the contract's penetration testing activities and issues. They must have the required years of experience listed in the labor category including and/or exceeding five (5) years of management and supervisory experience.

The NCO Technical Lead is responsible for leading the day-to-day technical oversight, coordination, and guidance to contractor personnel which includes performing operational cybersecurity and threat intelligence functions under the contract. They're responsible for leading all activities supporting the National Cybersecurity Operations (NCO) mission including threat intelligence, analysis and incident response(s) under the contract.

Key personnel may be drawn from the Offeror's staff or members of the Offeror's team if teaming arrangements are proposed. Certified resumes and letters of commitment from individuals not currently on staff are acceptable.

C.1.8.4 Volume IV: Price

The Offeror must use and complete the FAA's Price Model, an MS-Excel formatted file provided as Attachment B [Supplies and Services Prices]. All labor categories set out in Attachment B – the eFAST standard-level labor categories - defined in Section 4.1 of Attachment A [Statement of Work] – are mandatory and must be bid for the full 5-year period of performance. Note that all price information must reside in the Price Volume.

The FAA's best approximation of the annual labor requirement indicating estimated hours by labor category have been loaded into the Price Model. The Offeror's proposed annual labor rates will be multiplied by the FAA's approximated hours and summed over the 5-year period of performance to calculate the Offeror's Total Evaluated Cost (TEC).

Offerors are advised that the FAA's total estimated hours and their distribution across specific labor categories within the Price Model (see Attachment B for more details) may change as work is defined on individual task orders.

Offerors are reminded that the clause B.2 of the MOA governing this contract applies and must note that the labor rates in the MOA represent the maximum labor rates that the Government will consider or accept during the life of the award and no other labor rates will be recognized unless lower rates are offered nor may the Offeror propose any additional costs, such as so called 'pass through charges,' for work that is subcontracted.

C.1 Background
The Abrams Ground Vehicle Platform is adopting Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) to ensure the timing, accuracy, and reliability of critical data messages. Onboard computing devices are equipped with proprietary TSN configuration and testing software (e.g., GE Aerospace's Chronos & Helios) and are networked with TSN switches. To ensure successful integration and compliance with stringent performance requirements, government engineers require specialized training on these specific systems and standards.

C.2 Objective
The objective of this training is to provide Abrams program engineers with the in-depth knowledge and practical, hands-on skills required to effectively configure, manage, analyze, and test TSN networks on the Abrams Ground Vehicle Platform.

The NASA Interagency Implementation and Advanced Concepts Team (IMPACT) requires continued integration of NASA's specialized Earth science data, services, and tools into custom research applications and workflows. These capabilities enable the infusion of emerging trends in informatics, data science, and information technology to develop innovative solutions for science data management, dissemination, and rapid prototyping of advanced concepts in scientific data systems. The work includes addressing challenges related to data search, access, and usability. Specifically, the contractor will develop systematic approaches to data management and stewardship supporting distributed archive centers, provide system architecture analysis for IMPACT, and perform data curation for collaborations within NASA and with external partners, including other government agencies, industry, and academia. Although the present contract includes a 12-month Option Period 4 (October 1, 2025 – September 30, 2026), exercising that option is not in the Government's best interest. Option Period 4 contains tasks outside the current scope and does not align with the anticipated transition to the Next Gen contract. Therefore, extending Option Period 3 is the most efficient and cost-effective solution to maintain uninterrupted support.

1a) Federal EHR product modernization and deployment: This effort focuses on the full implementation of the Federal EHR at established, high-capacity treatment facilities.

Scope:
• One large accredited Outside Continental U.S. (OCONUS) medical center (250 beds), including two community hospitals (125 beds) and five associated out-patient clinics spread across multiple time zones with approximately 5,000 total users that includes foreign national employees and 50,000 beneficiaries.
• Services: Full-spectrum healthcare services are in scope, including inpatient, outpatient, acute, ambulatory, specialty, and dental care. This also includes all associated Revenue Cycle services such as billing, patient accounting, and referral management.
• Technical Environment: The solution must be integrated with a complex array of hardware, including medical devices and IT systems that require real-time, secure data exchange.

1b) OpMed products' modernization and deployment: This effort delivers mobile and interoperable OpMed solutions to medical forces in deployed and tactical environments in both the Continental U.S. (CONUS) and OCONUS. This includes theater hospitals, hospital ships, forward resuscitative sites, and submarines. These scalable deployments can occur concurrently across multiple sites, countries, or theaters, in remote or austere environments with each location receiving one or more products tailored to mission requirements. The deployment includes the OpMed product suite: Battlefield Assisted Trauma Distributed Observation Kit-Joint (BATDOK-J), Operational Medicine Care Delivery Platform (OpMed CDP), Theater Blood-Mobile (TBLD-M), and Medical Hub (MedHub).

1.0 Purpose
The purpose of this Statement of Work (SOW) is to secure a contractor to perform comprehensive maintenance and support for the Video Teleconferencing (VTC) and Audio/Visual (A/V) system located in building 1624, conference room 203, at 5 Eglin Street, Hanscom AFB, MA 01731. The contractor shall ensure the system remains fully functional through proactive maintenance, timely hardware replacement, on-call support, and user training.
2.0 Scope of Work
• Provide maintenance for a full audio visual and URL solution for one independent conference room that is capable of both classified and unclassified video teleconferencing (VTC) connectivity.
• Classified and unclassified VTC utilizes a single CODEC, automated periods processing hardware, and has NIPR/SIPR Teams capabilities.

Section VI - Evaluation and Award

RRB Supplement to FAR 52.212-2 Evaluation of Offers

A. The Government will award a contract resulting from this solicitation, based on price and technical factors including past performance, to the responsible offeror whose offer conforming to the solicitation will be most advantageous to the Government. Offerors proposals shall be evaluated in compliance with FAR 52.212-2, Evaluation of Offers – Commercial Items (Incorporated by Reference) and the procedures outlined in VI.B through VI.E. below.

Best Value Evaluation. Technical evaluation shall be weighted significantly greater than the Price evaluation. Responses will be evaluated according to which firm best can meet the performance requirements of the statement of work as evidenced by their Approach, Quality Control and Staffing qualifications. Past performance, value added characteristics, etc. will be included to determine which proposal is the best value to the government.

B. Business Price Proposal evaluation –
1. The Government will evaluate Offeror's Total Evaluated Price (TEP) proposal as the sum of the offered firm fixed prices in the sub-CLINs (AA and AB) of CLINs: 0001 thru 0005. Note: The RRB shall evaluate pricing on the above optional sub-CLINs whether or not they are exercised per the FAR clause 52.217-5, Evaluation of Options (Jul 1990).
3. The RRB will consider any Offer not proposing pricing in sub-CLINS of CLINs 0001 thru 0005 OR not being able to perform all the required elements in the SOW as described in Section III, as non-responsive to the solicitation and the RRB will not further evaluate such an offer.

VENDOR SHORT-LIST EVALUATION CRITERIA
The RFP selection committee may choose to interview one or all of the short-listed Vendors. Both the original submitted proposals and the results of any Vendor interviews and professional references will be used to select the final Vendor for the project. The following criteria are among those that will be used to evaluate the Vendors on the short-list:
1. Proposed Solution Functionality and Technical Design:
a. This criteria pertains to system functionality and design, including the experience of parking customers, and the City as the parking owner and operator. Aspects of the parking experience include accepting payment, event parking, navigating the devices, equipment, conducting enforcement, and collecting and reviewing data.
2. Proposed Approach approach/methodology completing the scope of services such as:
a. Logic and convenience of the proposed approach and methodology relative to the city's needs. Compatibility of the proposed approach and methodology to city policies and practices in administering this type of public improvement project.
b. Grasp of project requirements and level of interest in the project.
c. Creativity and problem-solving ability.
3. Experience, Qualifications, and References
a. Experience and qualifications of all team members (including any subconsultants) executing similar projects for municipalities similar to Dubuque, including in size, geographic market, weather conditions, and customer/user profile.
b. Responsiveness and compatibility between the Vendor and City, including general attitude and ability to communicate and be a high-quality team player, ability of the Vendor to maintain a high level of direct interaction and communication with city staff, ability to listen, be flexible, and follow and/or implement direction and/or ideas or concepts.
c. A Track record of all the vendor team members working together.
4. Ongoing System Maintenance and Support.
5. Proposed schedule required to complete project.
6. Cost of the proposed project.

I. OVERVIEW
The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), James A. Haley Veterans' Hospital, Tampa, FL, intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for the provision and installation of an Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)-compliant wayfinding signage system within Building 100, floors 1 through 3. This solicitation is issued as a Request for Quotation (RFQ) under FAR Part 13.

II. NAICS CODE AND SIZE STANDARD
NAICS Code: 339950 – Sign Manufacturing
Size Standard: 500 employees
This procurement is set aside for Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Businesses (SDVOSB) in accordance with VAAR 819.7005.

III. BUDGET AND PRICING
The total available funding for this contract is $55,000.00. This represents the maximum contract value and includes all labor, materials, fabrication, installation, project management, and cleanup. Quotations that exceed the stated budget ceiling will not be considered for award.

IV. PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
All work shall be completed within 60 calendar days after receipt of the Notice to Proceed. Work shall be performed during normal business hours (7:00 AM – 4:30 PM, Monday through Friday), excluding Federal holidays. After-hours work may be authorized by the COR on a case-by-case basis.

V. DELIVERY AND ACCEPTANCE
Final acceptance shall be contingent upon a joint walk-through with the COR, Facility Safety Officer, and the contractor to verify conformance with VA Signage Design Guide specifications and ADA Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG).

REPRESENTATION OF USE OF CLOUD COMPUTING (SEP 2015)
(a) Definition. "Cloud computing," as used in this provision, means a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. This includes other commercial terms, such as on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service. It also includes commercial offerings for software-as-a-service, infrastructure-as-a-service, and platform-as-a-service.

5.3 Architecture Overview – Hub-and-Spoke Model

UCO will implement a Hub-and-Spoke AI architecture where shared, centralized infrastructure powers multiple student-facing AI applications. Vendors must demonstrate their ability to design, deliver, and support this model.

Component | Purpose | Implementation Phase
The Hub | Core AI platform, system integrations, security framework, analytics, and data layer shared across all spokes. Includes secure AI Platform Infrastructure, Data Integration Framework, ML templates, centralized Security & Privacy Framework, and Ethical AI Governance. | Phase 1
Spoke 0 — Sentinel | Safety and compliance layer monitoring all AI interactions for harmful content, crisis signals, policy violations, and PII. Provides real-time escalation to UCO counseling and campus police. | Phase 1
Spoke 1 — Admissions & Career | AI-driven prospective student engagement, application guidance, financial aid information, career-to-program matching, skills gap analysis, and Oklahoma labor market alignment. | Phase 1
Spoke 2 — AI Success Buddy | Proactive academic advising support for enrolled students — degree planning, at-risk student outreach, course sequencing, and seamless human advisor handoff. | Phase 2

The hub infrastructure must be designed to support a minimum of 1,000 concurrent users, achieve 99.5% or greater uptime in production, and maintain consistent response latency for AI-served queries.

SECOND STEP – Proposal Peer or Scientific Review Criteria: Proposals will be reviewed through a Peer or Scientific Review process.

The following are the standard Proposal Peer or Scientific Review Criteria. The technical aspect, which is ranked as the first order of priority, shall be reviewed based on the following criteria that are of descending order of importance:

(1) Technical:
(a) Unique and innovative approach proposed to accomplish the technical objectives. New and creative solutions and/or advances in knowledge, understanding, technology, and the state of the art.
(b) The offeror's understanding of the scope of the technical effort.
(c) Soundness of the offeror's technical approach including whether the proposal identifies major technical risks, clearly defines feasible mitigation efforts, and demonstrates related experience and qualifications of technical personnel.
(d) The potential to transition the research and development deliverables to future Government needs. Any proposed restriction on technical data or computer software will be considered.

(2) Cost/Price: The cost evaluation includes the realism of the proposed cost. Cost/Price is a substantial factor but ranked as the second order of priority.

TRAC2ES is a critical system for the DoW supporting global patient movement operations by integrating medical regulating, patient evacuation, and command and control decision support, ensuring efficient and secure patient movement across peace, war, and contingency scenarios. TRAC2ES handles sensitive data, including Personal Identifiable Information (PII) and Protected Health Information (PHI), and operates within both classified and unclassified environments.

Problem Statement:

USTRANSCOM seeks innovative solutions to enhance, sustain, and modernize TRAC2ES. The solution must address challenges in scalability, performance, cybersecurity, interoperability, and operational efficiency while ensuring compliance with DoW standards. Additionally, the solution must include options for transitioning TRAC2ES functionality to a secure, user-friendly mobile application to improve accessibility and operational efficiency in diverse operational environments. The solution must optimize TRAC2ES's capabilities to meet evolving mission requirements while ensuring compliance with DoW standards, including Zero Trust (ZT) principles.

The CAML mission is to provide elements across all echelons with a mobile, autonomous/optionally crewed, versatile multi-domain fires capability and capacity far greater than current formation launcher and resupply capabilities. CAML reduces emplacement and displacement times, provides improved crew survivability, increases overall effectiveness, and allows commanders to weight the force appropriately during both offensive and defensive operations – enabling the ability to mass lethal assets and further dominate the operational environment across multiple domains.

The CAML System will be capable of Convoy (Manned Leader/Autonomous Follower), Autonomous Waypoint Following, Teleoperation, and Optionally Piloted / Manual modes of operation. The CAML system will operate in a tactical environment consisting of primary roads, secondary roads, and trails. The AMP will utilize Obstacle Detection and Obstacle Avoidance (ODOA) in all environments and weather conditions. The CAML System will support interface standards for integration of future autonomy solutions and future offensive and defensive munitions.

1. Background.

The Environmental Management Service (EMS) at the VA San Diego Healthcare System requires a customer feedback monitoring service to support restroom cleanliness monitoring and service response. The system will allow patients, visitors, and staff to provide real-time feedback regarding restroom conditions and notify EMS personnel when cleaning or supply services are required.

The objective is to improve restroom cleanliness, responsiveness to service issues, and overall patient experience through real-time data collection and reporting.

2. Scope.

The contractor shall provide a software-based restroom feedback monitoring service that collects real-time customer feedback data and alerts EMS personnel of restroom service needs.

The contractor shall provide all software, system hosting, communications services, data processing, reporting capability, and technical support necessary to operate the restroom feedback monitoring system.

The service shall support approximately 58 restroom feedback terminals currently installed throughout the VA San Diego Medical Center. The Government will provide the existing feedback terminals. The contractor shall ensure system connectivity, monitoring capability, and data reporting functionality.

The period of this Agreement shall consist of a series of Terms as defined below. The County is obligated only to pay such compensation under this Agreement as may lawfully be made from funds budgeted and appropriated for that purpose during the County's then current fiscal year.

The "Commencement Term" of this Agreement shall begin on July 1, 2026, the starting date, and shall end absolutely and without further obligation on the part of the County on June 30, 2027.

3.0 TERMS OF THE CONTRACT
3.1 Timeline
The customer intends to award based on deliverables. The Contractor must provide comprehensive, on-site and remote assistance to establish a secure cardholder data environment, remediate vulnerabilities, and empower local personnel to maintain long-term PCI compliance. Performance begins upon award of the contract and concludes 12 months after contract award with additional option years considered.

Period of Performance: 12 months, with option years as required.

3.2 Contractor Qualifications
A minimum of five (5) years of hands-on experience is required. AFSVC requires a technical support contractor with expertise in cybersecurity and a track record of successfully supporting and guiding IT teams in securing and monitoring networks and expanding their knowledge base. The ideal candidate must possess a strong background in network security and cybersecurity. Prior experience working with the Federal Government or DoW is highly desirable.

M.7 PRICE
The Courts will evaluate proposed pricing, including by contract line item, option year, and overall, for reasonableness, completeness, and price realism. The Courts will not rate or score price. The Courts will also evaluate proposals for price unbalancing, that is, whether the price of one or more CLINs is significantly over or understated (unbalanced pricing) so as to suggest increased performance risk or unreasonably high prices. Alternative price proposals, if considered by the Courts will be evaluated on contract type risk, potential savings, other advantages or disadvantages to the Courts, and the discretion of the government.
(a) Price Reasonableness. In evaluating price reasonableness, the Courts will determine if the Offeror's proposed prices, in nature and amount, do not exceed those which would be incurred by a prudent contractor in the conduct of competitive business. The assessment of price reasonableness will consider the context of the source selection, including current market conditions and related factors that may impact price. The evaluation will consider whether proposed pricing is comparable to the Courts' independent cost estimate and to competitors' prices.
(b) Completeness. In evaluating completeness, the Courts will determine if the Offeror provides data of sufficient detail to fully support the offer and permit the Courts to adequately evaluate pricing and assumptions. The Courts will assess the following: (i) Did the Offeror propose pricing for all CLINS and option years? (ii) Do proposed prices account for all requirements? (iii) Do the proposed prices include all price elements the offeror is likely to incur in performing the task or providing the deliverable?
(c) Price Realism. The Courts will evaluate whether low proposed pricing reflects an offeror's clear understanding of RFP requirements and is realistic to provide the required System and perform the required services.

• Provide secure web-based access that can be used from office and remote work environments.
• Provide individual user accounts with administrative controls for managing access and permissions.

Digital Code Library
The platform shall include:
• Searchable access to current state adopted building codes, including building, fire, residential, mechanical, plumbing, and energy codes as applicable.
• Regular updates to ensure codes reflect the most current adopted versions and amendments.
• Tools to quickly search, filter, and navigate code sections.
• Cross-referencing between related code sections and standards.

Research and Collaboration Tools
The platform should include tools that support plan review and internal collaboration, such as:
• Ability to highlight, annotate, and save notes within code sections.
• Ability to create shared projects or folders for plan review references.
• Ability to share notes or comments among team members.
• Tools that support consistent code review comments and documentation.

The District's current Customer Water Usage Portal provided by Sensus is being discontinued, and a new portal is needed. The current portal allows the District's customers to monitor their hourly water use and set up alerts for high or continuous usage, which supports the District's conservation messaging. Approximately 1,500 District customers currently use the portal. The District invites proposals from Vendors offering Commercial off-the-shelf software solutions as well as from Vendors proposing to design and implement a custom-developed customer portal tailored to the District's requirements.

The District has prepared this Request for Proposals (RFP) to solicit Vendors (Proposers) to propose a new Customer Portal Solution for the District's 28,000 customer accounts. The District currently utilizes the Sensus Customer Portal, which is being discontinued in August 2026. To continue to provide customer access to their detailed water use data, a replacement is needed.

The District currently uses the Sensus AMI System with towers that read all retail meters and provide hourly use data over the air. The data is stored in the Sensus Analytics Platform. The District uses Xpress Bill Pay for customer payments.

EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD – GENERAL PROCEDURES

A. The Government will evaluate proposals using a tradeoff process to determine which offerors represent the Best Value to the Government, as defined in FAR 2.101 and described in FAR 52.212-2. The Government may award to other than the lowest priced offeror or to other than the proposal assessed the most non-price merit. The relative importance considering all evaluation factors is provided as follows: all evaluation factors other than cost or price, when combined, are significantly more important than cost or price.

B. The Government estimates up to four (4) awards will be made under this solicitation. This estimate in no way prohibits the Government from making either fewer or more awards than estimated based upon the offers received and a determination of the best interests of the Government.

C. The Government may award without discussions with Offerors in accordance with FAR 52.215-1. Therefore, the Offeror's initial proposal should contain the Offerors' best terms from a technical and price standpoint. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if deemed necessary.

5.1 Overview – Current Environment

Currently, student engagement across the Admissions and Academic Advising lifecycle relies on a combination of manual processes and siloed digital tools. UCO aims to transition these legacy workflows into a human-in-the-loop AI-native environment, providing AI direct engagement to students via AI tools that act as a guide through the UCO experience — from initial visit to UCO.edu through their entire academic journey. This transformation will allow faculty and staff to leverage AI for routine tasks while focusing their expertise on high-impact student interactions, thereby enhancing the overall customer service experience for every student.

UCO's vision is not simply a chatbot — it is a full IT ecosystem. At the center is a unified AI Core Platform that manages security, identity, and data, mirrors modern enterprise contact center architectures, and ensures that AI resolves the routine student queries while seamlessly routing the complex questions to the right human experts.

Thus, CIRCLE will be composed of two types of performers: 1) integrated teams each working on all three TAs, and 2) no more than three performers carrying out support and Independent Verification and Validation (IV&V) roles in three separate "APs" (AP1, AP2 and AP3) that are paired with the corresponding TAs: TA1, TA2 and TA3 (See Figure 1 and sections 1.5.2 and 1.6.4 below).

Each proposal responsive to this ISO may be of one of two types: they must either address all three TAs, "Type A", or address a single AP component, "Type B". CIRCLE is seeking a diversity of approaches for each TA, and therefore individuals and corporate units are discouraged from contributing to more than one Type A funded effort. Additionally, to avoid conflicts of interest between TA performers and AP performers, no organization, whether acting as a primary or sub-performer, may participate in both a Type A and a Type B proposal.

Proposals must address all three TAs. The proposing organization must designate an individual or individuals (typically the proposal Project Lead (PL) working with one or more administrative managers) to have the primary role of Team Integrator to coordinate the activities of groups dedicated to the three TAs, and assure that milestones are met and deliverables delivered, whether these groups are components of the proposing organization or sub-performers. All proposals must include appropriate effort on the part of the PL to coordinate each TA with AP/IV&V partners (AP1, AP2, and AP3) as indicated in sections 1.6.3, and 1.6.4 below.

Multiple awards are anticipated for TA team (Type A) performers to assure sufficient diversity in measurement, modeling, and therapeutic modulation, as well as a diversity of different etiologies of critical illness from different hospitals and regions to foster a variety of solutions that reflect the U.S. healthcare system.

Electronic copies of proposals are to be submitted in Adobe Acrobat (PDF) format. Proposals shall be submitted in three separate volumes. Technical/Management should be submitted in separate volumes/folders/files with Business and Past Performance Information Proposals via PDF format (No hard copies accepted) electronically email to the Contracting Officer.

Volume I - Technical/Management Proposal: Offeror's Performance Work Statement/Proposal. NO PRICING information shall be in Volume I.

Volume II - Business Proposal: Sections A, B, J, and K. To include completed copies of the Business Management Questionnaire, Bank Reference Letters, Secure Software Development Attestation Form and the Small Business Subcontracting Plan.

Volume III - Past Performance Information. Completed Past Performance Questionnaires and a list of current and previous contracts.

L.5 TPS Content of Performance Work Statement (PWS)/Proposal
a. An offeror's Technical Proposal is a critical consideration in the Government's award of a contract. The Statement of Objectives (SOO) provide the Government's overall objectives and performance requirements for this solicitation. Offerors shall use the SOO with other applicable portions of this RFP, as the basis for preparing their proposal. The offeror shall ensure all aspects of the SOO are addressed. The PWS should specify in clear, understandable terms the work to be done in developing the services to be performed by the contractor. Preparation of an effective PWS requires both the understanding of the services that are needed to satisfy a particular requirement and an ability to define what is required in specific, quantitative terms.

Best Value/Tradeoff Source Selection Procedures -
The Government intends to award a Firm Fixed Price (FFP) Purchase Order contract to the responsible offeror whose proposal represents the best value after evaluation in accordance with the criteria of the solicitation, considering non-price and price factors. The Government intends to evaluate proposals and award a contract based on initial proposals. Accordingly, the offeror should submit its most favorable terms from a performance approach, management approach, and past performance and price standpoint. The Government reserves the right to award a contract to other than the lowest priced offeror or other than the highest technically rated proposal; award a contract without discussions; or award a contract after discussions, whichever is in the best interest of the Government.

All proposals will be reviewed for timeliness. Timely proposals will be reviewed for compliance to the solicitation. If the proposal is untimely or not compliant to the solicitation, it will not be evaluated, and the offeror will be notified that the proposal is non-responsive. Offerors simply providing general statements or paraphrasing/parroting the PWS in whole or in any part, without properly detailing methodology, shall result in a rating of unacceptable.

The evaluation of proposals will consider the Non-Price (technical) Proposal to be more important than the Price Proposal. Within the Non-Price Proposal offerors must receive an Acceptable rating for the Phase I Factor to be further considered for Phase II evaluation. The following Phase II Factors are in descending order of importance: Performance Approach, Management Approach, Past Performance.

Although price is not the most important evaluation factor, it has the potential to become more significant during the evaluation process.

1.3.1 On-site Program Manager
1.3.1.1 It is mandatory that the contractor provides at a minimum one (1) full-time on-site Program Manager (PM) to oversee and direct the contractor employees.
1.3.1.2 The PM will be responsible for learning the basic responsibilities of the three positions: Test Examiner, OPI/Web-based Test Scheduler and OPI Facilitator.
1.3.1.3 The PM will coordinate daily with both the section supervisors and the COR.
1.3.1.4 The PM must complete all orientation for their position and all orientations and certifications required for the Test Examiner position.
1.3.1.5 The PM will be present during the initial orientation of all employees.
1.3.1.6 The PM will also fill the position of OPI Facilitator when required.

1.3.2 Test Examiner:
1.3.2.1 The Test Examiner will administer, or proctor tests as directed by the PM. Each test session is defined as one AM or PM test session. The government will determine the number of sessions required each week. The number of sessions per week will not exceed 50. The COR will notify the contractor with the intended schedule no later than 14 days prior to the designated work unit. The maximum number of sessions in a year would be 2500 and the minimum 1250.

3. White Paper/ Proposal Review Information

a. FIRST STEP - White Paper Peer or Scientific Review Criteria: Those offerors submitting White Papers as assessed as meeting Air Force needs will be asked to submit a Proposal (Second Step). Offerors not selected to proceed will be notified of the disposition of their White Paper. The Government will review White Papers to determine which of them have the potential to best meet the Air Force's needs based on the following criteria, which are listed in descending order of importance:

1) Unique and innovative approach proposed to accomplish the technical objectives. New and creative solutions and/or advances in knowledge, understanding, technology, and the state of the art.
2) The offeror's understanding of the scope of the technical effort.
3) Soundness of the offeror's technical approach.
4) Affordability (Proposed ROM Cost Estimate).

b. SECOND STEP - Proposal Peer or Scientific Review Criteria: Proposals will be reviewed through a Peer or Scientific Review process.

The following are the standard Proposal Peer or Scientific Review Criteria. The technical aspect, which is ranked as the first order of priority, shall be reviewed based on the following criteria that are of descending order of importance:

(1) Technical:
(a) Unique and innovative approach proposed to accomplish the technical objectives. New and creative solutions and/or advances in knowledge, understanding, technology, and the state of the art.
(b) The offeror's understanding of the scope of the technical effort.
(c) Soundness of the offeror's technical approach including whether the proposal identifies major technical risks, clearly defines feasible mitigation efforts, and demonstrates related experience and qualifications of technical personnel.
(d) The potential to transition the research and development deliverables to future Government needs. Any proposed restriction on technical data or computer software will be considered.

(2) Cost/Price: The cost evaluation includes the realism of the proposed cost. Cost/Price is a substantial factor, but ranked as the second order of priority.

Scoring Weights: Proposal scores will be based on a 100-point scale and will measure the degree to which each proposal meets the following criteria. The proposal materials that will be evaluated under each section below are identified in Part IV of the RFP. Scoring Process: For proposals that demonstrate meeting the eligibility requirements in Section I, if applicable, the evaluation team will use a consensus approach to evaluate and score Sections II & III above. Members of the evaluation team will not score those sections individually but, instead, will arrive at a consensus as to assignment of points for each of those sections. Section IV, the Cost Proposal, will be scored as described below. Scoring the Cost Proposal: The total cost proposed for conducting all the functions specified in the RFP will be assigned a score according to a mathematical formula. The proposal with the lowest total fixed least cost will be awarded 25 points. Proposals with higher bid values will be awarded proportionately fewer points calculated in comparison with the lowest bid. The scoring formula is: (Lowest submitted total fixed lease cost / total fixed lease cost of proposal being scored) x 25 = pro-rated score. No Best and Final Offers: The State of Maine will not seek or accept a best and final offer (BAFO) from any Bidder in this procurement process. All Bidders are expected to provide their best value pricing with the submission of their proposal.

6. SYSTEM INTEROPERABILITY
Solutions of interest should be capable of integration with existing Federal procurement and grants systems, including but not limited to the Federal Procurement Data System (FPDS), www.SAM.gov, and agency contract writing or grants management systems.
Application Programming Interface (API) based or structured data exchange capabilities are strongly preferred to enable scalability and long-term sustainability.
7. DATA GOVERNANCE AND SECURITY
Solutions should enable:
a) Traceability and auditability sufficient to support waiver review and Inspector General oversight;
b) Clear documentation of data sources and validation methodologies;
c) Secure handling of confidential, proprietary, or national security-sensitive information; and
d) Mechanisms for continuous updating to reflect evolving manufacturing landscapes.
Respondents are encouraged to describe proposed data ownership structures, licensing terms, and any restrictions on Government use of aggregated data.

The purpose of this synopsis is to announce AFDW-PK, Joint Base Andrews, MD 20762, anticipates awarding a Hybrid (Labor) Firm-Fixed Price (FFP) & T&M (ODCs) Tools and Travel, A&AS contract for: One (1) year base with two (2) one-year option periods, IAW with FAR 52.217-9 (Option to Extend the term of the Contract). The contract will include a six (6) month extension IAW FAR 52.217-8 (Option to Extend Services). This contract action will be a follow-on for Systems Engineering Technical Advisory Support (SETA) for DoD Cyber Crime Center (DC3), that requires specialized technical support to enhance its ability to investigate and prevent criminal and foreign malicious activity, as well as protect Department of the Air Force resources and personnel. Period of Performance: Base Year: 12 months (6 Sep 2026 – 5 Sep 2027); Option Period 1: 12 months (6 Sep 2027 – 5 Sep 2028); Option Period 2: 12 months (6 Sep 2028 – 5 Sep 2029); Option to Extend Services (FAR 52.217-8): 6 months (6 Sep 2029 – 5 Mar 2030).

2. Best Value Criteria
The Cooperative evaluates Proposals on the basis of best value to Cooperative members. In evaluating Proposals and determining best value for all Cooperative members, the Cooperative will consider the following criteria:

Best Value Criteria Maximum Points
1 Price Competitiveness 45
2 Vendor Past Performance 15
3 Vendor Reputation for Goods and Services 15
4 Financial and Technical Resources 15
5 Capability of Servicing Cooperative Members 5
6 Any other relevant factor or requirement listed in this Proposal Invitation 5
TOTAL 100

The Cooperative's evaluation may include Vendor's responses to the forms and other attachments or information included with a Proposal or associated with this Proposal Invitation, including but not limited to Vendor's responses to the Proposal Invitation Questionnaire, as well as any other information at its disposal deemed relevant by the Cooperative in its sole discretion. Only responsive Proposals will be evaluated. Failure to include all required information may result in a Proposal being deemed non-responsive.

As a general matter, the Cooperative seeks to make competitive and indefinite quantity awards to Proposers that give the same or better discounts/pricing than they give their best governmental clients and can provide best value to Cooperative members.

2.0 TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS
2.a. Source Code Management - Integration with Git for distributed version control, branching, and pull request workflows.
2.b. Infrastructure as Code (IaC) - Support for tools like Terraform or Ansible to manage infrastructure through code, ensuring consistency.
2.c. Containerization - Support for container technologies such as Docker and Kubernetes for orchestration.
2.d. Automated Testing - Integration of automated test frameworks within the CI/CD pipeline.
2.e. Manual Testing - Capabilities to manage and execute manual test cases, such as Azure Test Plans.
2.f. Security Scanning - Automated security analysis (DevSecOps) within the pipeline.
2.g. Observability - Integrated monitoring to provide feedback on application performance in production.
2.h. Telemetry - Tools to monitor deployment success rates and application health.
2.i. Automated testing tool - Native automated testing tools.
2.j. Primary and secondary hosting - Prefer (Primary) East coast and Secondary Mid-west (low latency and high accessibility).
